sections.headline: Faster competency. Higher engagement. Agentic Learning with Scholé.

sections.headline: Your first lesson takes 2 minutes
sections.body: Start free, no credit card required. Tell Olé your role and tools, and it builds your path.

currentVariants.name: ROI-first for the L&D director
currentVariants.thesis: Every variant bled out at the hero with roi_proof unresolved (402 lost on v3 alone, #1 or #2 in all six) because the content that answers it — features (v1 sentiment 1.17), tour (v5 sentiment 1.41), and proof — sat below heroes most visitors abandoned, and the CTA was functionally unreachable (single-digit views everywhere). This page makes one bet: pull quantified ROI and the strongest emotional peaks forward, anchor everything on the ld_director (the most reliable buyer: v4 10/238, v1 8/289, v0 5/187) rather than diffusing across six personas, and resolve roi_proof, employee_adoption, and time_cost in the top third where traffic actually exists. It keeps v3's winning problem (sentiment 0.63) and how (0.58) sections, hoists a v1-style features peak, and puts a low-friction demo ask in reach because satisfied LOST visitors repeatedly said 'addressed 2/2, I'd book a demo' but never reached the CTA.

changelog.what: Rewrote the hero to lead with quantified ROI and the license-waste frame, and folded features/dashboard proof-points directly into it.
changelog.why: roi_proof is the #1 or #2 unresolved objection in every variant and the proof section that answers it is starved of traffic; the fix is to answer it above the fold where visitors actually are.
changelog.evidence: roi_proof lost 402 on v3; proof views collapse to 31-96 of hundreds-to-thousands of visits (v1 96/1392, v0 31/740).

changelog.what: Kept the problem and how sections nearly intact and moved them into the top of the page.
changelog.why: These were the strongest-read sections on the parent and should be preserved and given more traffic.
changelog.evidence: v3 problem scored sentiment 0.63 / readRate 0.31 and how scored sentiment 0.58 / readRate 0.29 — the two best content sections on the parent.

changelog.what: Hoisted a v1-style features section high and rewrote it to pair adaptivity with reporting the director can defend.
changelog.why: Features is the emotional peak of the dataset but is buried below weak heroes, so its value never reaches most visitors.
changelog.evidence: v1 features scored sentiment 1.17 at readRate 0.44 — the best-read section anywhere — yet only 270/1392 (19%) saw it; v3 features scored 0.89.

changelog.what: Cut the press/credibility section and the standalone product tour.
changelog.why: Press read at 0.00 with negative sentiment and high exit; the tour underperformed on the parent, so both were removed to shorten the path to the CTA.
changelog.evidence: v3 press: readRate 0.00, sentiment -0.31, exit 60%; v3 tour: sentiment 0.13, exit 40%.

changelog.what: Rewrote the CTA to a lower-friction 'bring your numbers' demo ask and moved it within reach by trimming to seven sections.
changelog.why: Satisfied visitors left without converting because the CTA was functionally unreachable; friction sat between intent and the ask.
changelog.evidence: LOST visitors repeatedly said 'addressed 2/2 of my critical concerns. I'd book a demo'; CTA views were single-digit across variants (v3=8, v1=11, v0=3).

changelog.what: Anchored all messaging on the L&D director rather than diffusing across six personas, keeping the demo-gated CTA over self-serve.
changelog.why: The L&D director is the most reliable buyer, and self-serve CTAs unlock learners but sacrifice buyer conversion.
changelog.evidence: ld_director converts best (v4 10/238, v1 8/289, v0 5/187); v5's self-serve CTA dropped overall conversion to 1.9% (16/835) with ld_director at 3/190.

sections.headline: Your AI licenses are active. Your ROI isn't — yet.
sections.body: You bought ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, Claude. The subscriptions renew every month. Ask most employees what changed in their daily work and the answer is: not much. Scholé turns those licenses into measurable adoption — role-specific paths, 2-minute lessons inside the workday, and an HR dashboard that shows completion, mastery, and adoption from day one. Built for the L&D leader who has to defend the spend.

Meanwhile the licenses renew, and the productivity gains stay theoretical. That gap is the number you're accountable for.

currentVariants.name: ROI-first for the L&D buyer (front-loaded value)
currentVariants.ctaGoal: Book a 20-minute founder demo (L&D/HR buyer), anchored on the ROI dashboard
currentVariants.thesis: Every parent bled out at the hero with roi_proof unresolved as the #1-2 killer in all six variants, while the content that actually converts—v1's features (sentiment 1.17, readRate 0.44) and the proof/dashboard sections—sat buried below heroes that 38-62% of visitors abandoned, seen by only 13-19% of traffic. This page makes one bet: hoist v1-roi's winning ROI-defense hero (the only positive-sentiment, lowest-exit hero at 0.45/38%) to the top, then immediately front-load the quantified-adoption features and Decathlon dashboard proof that landed hardest, so the L&D director (the most reliable buyer: v4 10/238, v1 8/289, v0 5/187) hits proof of measurable ROI before any drop-off point instead of after it. Because satisfied visitors repeatedly left saying 'addressed my concerns, I'd book a demo' yet the CTA drew single-digit views, we also shorten the arc to 7 sections and carry v1's high-performing CTA (readRate 0.55, sentiment 1.08) into reach, keeping the ask demo-gated to protect buyer conversion rather than diffusing to self-serve.

changelog.what: Promoted v1's ROI-defense hero ('Stop reporting completion rates. Start proving AI adoption.') to lead the page over baseline and problem-first heroes.
changelog.why: Hero quality directly predicts downstream survival and conversion; only this hero was net-positive and low-exit, while roi_proof was the dominant unresolved killer everywhere.
changelog.evidence: v1's hero scored sentiment 0.45, exit 38% — the best hero in the set — and carried 833 visitors to 'how' and the most conversions (34); negative heroes collapsed (v0 -0.21/50%, v4 -0.12/51%, v2 -0.31/62%/0 conv).

changelog.what: Front-loaded the quantified-adoption features section into position 3, near the top instead of buried mid-page.
changelog.why: Features is the emotional peak of the page but was gated behind weak heroes, so almost no one experienced the content that actually converts.
changelog.evidence: v1 features scored sentiment 1.17 at readRate 0.44 — the best-read section in the dataset — yet only 270/1392 (19%) ever reached it.

changelog.what: Kept the Decathlon/Harvard proof but reframed it around the business-oriented HR dashboard and placed it high to answer roi_proof directly.
changelog.why: The old proof section was starved of traffic and landed flat-to-negative; pairing proof with the dashboard reframe ties it to the objection it must resolve.
changelog.evidence: Proof views collapsed to 31/740 (v0) and 96/1392 (v1) with sentiment often negative (v0 proof -0.21, v5 -0.02), whereas v1's dashboard-framed proof scored sentiment 0.88 at readRate 0.33.

changelog.what: Imported v3's 'meet employees where they already are' how-it-works section for the adoption/time-cost narrative.
changelog.why: This was one of the strongest-sentiment how sections and resolves the consistent #2 objection, employee_adoption, plus time_cost.
changelog.evidence: v3's how scored sentiment 0.58 at readRate 0.29 with exit 33%, well above v1's how (-0.08) and v0's how (-0.11).

changelog.what: Cut the standalone press/credibility section entirely.
changelog.why: It was the single worst-performing content block across parents — near-zero read, strongly negative sentiment, high exit — adding nothing to the arc.
changelog.evidence: Press scored readRate 0.00 / sentiment -0.31 / exit 60% (v3), and readRate 0.08 / sentiment -0.52 (v0); v2's AI-Act-first framing produced 0 conversions across 153 visits.

changelog.what: Shortened the page to 7 sections and carried v1's high-performing CTA into reach, keeping the ask demo-gated rather than self-serve.
changelog.why: Satisfied visitors were leaving before an unreachable CTA; shortening the arc gets intent to the ask, and demo-gating protects the reliable buyer instead of diluting to learners.
changelog.evidence: CTA views were single digits everywhere (v1=11, v3=8, v0=3) despite v1's CTA scoring readRate 0.55 / sentiment 1.08; v5's self-serve CTA lifted learners but dropped overall conversion to 1.9% and buyer conversion (ld_director 3/190).

sections.headline: Stop reporting completion rates. Start proving AI adoption.
sections.body: Scholé's HR dashboard turns AI training into numbers your CFO respects: adoption rate, mastery level, and time to skill for every team and every tool, trackable in natural language from day one. No more proving a training budget with certificates nobody reads.

sections.headline: Completion certificates don't survive a budget meeting.
sections.body: Most L&D teams can show who finished a course. Almost none can show who changed how they work. That gap is why learning budgets get cut, and why L&D is not in the room when priorities get set. Meanwhile the ChatGPT, Copilot, and Gemini licenses renew, and the productivity gains stay theoretical.

sections.headline: Insights your LMS admin can't give you
sections.body: Ask questions in plain language and get answers about real adoption for every team and every tool, ready before your next QBR. Every employee follows a path built around their actual tools and role, so the metrics reflect real work, not seat time.

items.title: Adapts in real time
items.detail: Lesson content adjusts as each learner progresses, so no one is stuck or bored.

items.title: Content tied to daily work
items.detail: Every lesson maps to the learner's actual tools — Notion, Excel, PowerPoint — and job function.

items.title: Measurable outcomes
items.detail: Completion rates, mastery levels, and adoption metrics, trackable in natural language from day one.

sections.headline: Meet employees where they already are
sections.body: Scholé's multi-agent engine builds each employee's path around their actual role and daily tools like Notion, Excel, PowerPoint, and Slack. Two-minute micro-lessons that fit inside the workday — so adoption happens without workload becoming the excuse not to train.

sections.headline: Bring numbers to your next budget meeting
sections.body: 20 minutes, live with a founder. We'll show the dashboard on data like yours and leave you with a metric framework you can use either way.

currentVariants.name: ROI-First, Front-Loaded (v6)
currentVariants.ctaGoal: Book a 20-minute demo with a founder (L&D director as primary buyer)
currentVariants.thesis: Every Gen-0 variant bled out at a weak hero while its best content (features, tour) stayed buried below the fold and the proof section that should answer roi_proof was starved of traffic (v0 proof: 31/740 views, sentiment -0.21). This page makes one bet: hoist quantified ROI, adaptive-features value, and social proof into the top three sections, anchored on the ld_director buyer who converts most reliably (v0 5/187, v1 8/289, v4 10/238). We keep v0's high-sentiment features section (0.54 / readRate 0.30) but move it up, rewrite the negative hero (-0.21, 50% exit) into an ROI-forward promise, cut the press section that landed worst anywhere (sentiment -0.52), and place a demo CTA far earlier because satisfied LOST visitors repeatedly said 'I'd book a demo' but never reached a CTA (v0 cta views: 3). The strategic wager is that resolving roi_proof visibly and early — not repositioning adjectives — is what converts the buyer who was already leaving satisfied.

changelog.what: Rewrote the hero from an abstract 'Faster competency. Higher engagement.' promise into an ROI-and-adoption-forward hook aimed at the L&D buyer.
changelog.why: The v0 hero repelled visitors (sentiment -0.21, exit 50%) and roi_proof was the #1 unresolved objection costing 470 conversions; hero sentiment predicts downstream survival.
changelog.evidence: v0 hero sentiment -0.21, exit 50%, 19 conv; roi_proof(470) top unresolved objection. Analyst: 'Any page that hoisted features/tour proof-points and quantified ROI into the hero would likely outperform all six.'

changelog.what: Added a new 'outcomes' section as section two, front-loading measurable ROI (dashboard, adoption metrics, natural-language reporting).
changelog.why: roi_proof is the universal conversion killer and was never resolved because the proof content was starved of traffic; putting quantified ROI high answers it before visitors exit.
changelog.evidence: roi_proof #1/#2 unresolved in all six variants; proof views collapsed to 31/740 (v0). Analyst: 'roi_proof is the universal, dominant conversion killer and it is never resolved.'

changelog.what: Moved the features section from position 4 up to position 3, keeping its content nearly verbatim.
changelog.why: Features was the emotional peak of the page but buried below a hero most people abandoned; hoisting it exposes the best-read content to far more visitors.
changelog.evidence: v0 features readRate 0.30, sentiment 0.54, exit 32% — best content section in v0. Analyst: v1 features 'best-read section in the entire dataset' seen by only 19%.

changelog.what: Cut the [press] credibility section entirely and folded EPFL/UC Berkeley credibility into the hero line.
changelog.why: The press section was the single worst-performing section anywhere and added no conversion value.
changelog.evidence: v0 press sentiment -0.52, readRate 0.08, exit 54% — lowest sentiment section in v0.

changelog.what: Removed the EU AI Act framing from hero/CTA and demoted it to a single FAQ line.
changelog.why: AI-Act-first framing repelled every persona and produced a categorical failure in the variant that led with it.
changelog.evidence: v2-compliance: 0 conversions across 153 visits, 99% bounce, hero sentiment -0.31, exit 62%; failed even compliance_lead 0/14.

changelog.what: Placed the demo CTA earlier in the flow and kept the ask as 'book a demo' anchored on the L&D director rather than a self-serve 'start for free.'
changelog.why: Satisfied LOST visitors said they'd book a demo but never reached a CTA (single-digit CTA views); ld_director is the most reliable buyer and self-serve sacrificed buyer conversion.
changelog.evidence: CTA views: v0=3, v1=11, v3=8. v3 LOST ld_directors: 'addressed 2/2... I'd book a demo.' ld_director converts best (v4 10/238, v1 8/289). v5 self-serve dropped overall conv to 1.9%.

changelog.what: Trimmed to 7 sections and cut the low-value 'how_it_works' and 'problem' sections, merging their intent into hero and features.
changelog.why: Both scored negative sentiment and high exit in v0 while duplicating the adoption-gap message now carried by the hero and outcomes sections.
changelog.evidence: v0 how sentiment -0.11 exit 52%; v0 problem sentiment -0.27 exit 58% — two of the weakest sections in the parent.

sections.headline: Turn the AI tools you already pay for into measurable adoption — with proof in the dashboard
sections.body: You bought ChatGPT, Copilot, and Gemini. Scholé makes your teams actually use them, and gives L&D leaders the completion, mastery, and adoption metrics to prove the return. Built on 10+ years of AI-for-education research from EPFL and UC Berkeley.

sections.headline: What L&D leaders can measure from day one
sections.body: Scholé is built around the metrics a director defends to their exec team. Completion rates, mastery levels, and adoption are trackable in natural language from the first cohort — the same HR dashboard Decathlon Switzerland's People Development lead called 'one of the most business-oriented I have encountered.'

sections.headline: Ready to turn AI tools into real, measurable adoption?
sections.body: You've seen the outcomes, the adaptive engine, and the customers. If you're an L&D leader who needs to prove AI return, spend 20 minutes live with a founder and see the adoption dashboard on your own use case.

currentVariants.name: ROI-front, features-hoisted (ld_director anchor)
currentVariants.ctaGoal: Book a 20-minute founder demo (ld_director / L&D buyer), CTA reachable within a short 7-section arc while ROI intent is warm
currentVariants.thesis: Every parent bled out at the hero with roi_proof unresolved (402-625 lost per variant), while the two highest-sentiment sections in the entire dataset — v1 features (1.17 @ 0.44 readRate) and the product tour — sat buried below weak heroes where only 13-19% of visitors ever saw them. This page makes one bet: hoist the dashboard/ROI proof that L&D directors need to defend budget INTO the hero and immediately after, so the emotional peak is front-loaded rather than gated. It borrows v1-roi's winning hero (sentiment 0.45, exit 38%, most conversions) and its best-in-set features section, keeps v3's strong problem-agitation numbers (readRate 0.31, sentiment 0.63), and drops the press/credibility strip that scored -0.31 to -0.52 everywhere. Because satisfied LOST visitors repeatedly said 'addressed my concerns, I'd book a demo' but never reached a single-digit-view CTA, the page shortens the arc to 7 sections and lands the demo ask while intent is still warm, anchored on ld_director — the most reliable buyer across variants (v4 10/238, v1 8/289).

changelog.what: Adopted v1-roi's dashboard hero ('Stop reporting completion rates. Start proving AI adoption.') as the page hero.
changelog.why: roi_proof is the #1/#2 unresolved objection in every variant and hero quality predicts downstream survival; front-loading the ROI answer attacks the dominant conversion killer at first contact.
changelog.evidence: v1's hero was the best in set (sentiment 0.45, exit 38%) and carried the most visitors deep (833 to how, most conversions at 34), while negative heroes collapsed (v0 -0.21/50%/19 conv, v2 -0.31/62%/0 conv).

changelog.what: Hoisted v1's features/dashboard-insights section to third position, directly after the problem.
changelog.why: The highest-value content converts but was architecturally back-loaded below weak heroes; moving it above the fold exposes the emotional peak to far more traffic.
changelog.evidence: v1 features scored sentiment 1.17 at readRate 0.44 — the best-read section in the dataset — yet only 270/1392 (19%) ever reached it.

changelog.what: Merged v3's problem-section adoption statistics into a single hard-hitting problem section (83% vs 64%, half the workforce, licenses renewing).
changelog.why: Keep what worked: v3's problem framing had strong dwell and sentiment and reinforces roi_proof + employee_adoption, the consistent #1 and #2 objections.
changelog.evidence: v3 problem scored readRate 0.31, sentiment 0.63, exit 37%; roi_proof cost 402 conversions and employee_adoption 123 on v3.

changelog.what: Kept the how_it_works 'meet employees where they already are' framing from v3.
changelog.why: It substantively resolves relevance_to_role and time_cost with concrete tool/workday specifics rather than generic 'multi-agent engine' copy that scored negative.
changelog.evidence: v3 how scored sentiment 0.58 / readRate 0.29 / exit 33%, versus v0/v1's generic 'Learn the new way' how at sentiment -0.08 to -0.11 and exit 47-52%.

changelog.what: Cut the [press]/credibility strip and the [faq] section entirely.
changelog.why: Both were consistent sentiment sinks with near-zero read rates and inflated the arc, pushing the CTA out of reach; removing them shortens the path to the demo ask.
changelog.evidence: press scored sentiment -0.31 to -0.52 and readRate 0.00-0.14 across v0/v1/v3; faq hit readRate 0.00 and sentiment -0.52 on v0.

changelog.what: Shortened the page to 7 sections ending in v1's high-sentiment 'Bring numbers to your next budget meeting' CTA.
changelog.why: Satisfied visitors were leaving because the CTA was functionally unreachable; a shorter arc lands the demo ask while intent is warm rather than gating it behind sections almost no one reaches.
changelog.evidence: CTA views were single digits everywhere (v3=8, v1=11, v0=3) despite v1's CTA scoring sentiment 1.08 at readRate 0.55; LOST visitors repeatedly said 'addressed my critical concerns, I'd book a demo' yet never converted.

changelog.what: Anchored all messaging on the L&D director / buyer rather than diffusing across six personas or leaning self-serve.
changelog.why: ld_director is the most reliable buyer and a focused ROI-defense narrative avoids the buyer-conversion dilution seen in self-serve and multi-persona variants.
changelog.evidence: ld_director converts best across variants (v4 10/238, v1 8/289, v0 5/187); v5's self-serve pitch lifted learners but dropped overall conversion to 1.9% and ld_director to 3/190.

sections.headline: Stop reporting completion rates. Start proving AI adoption.
sections.body: Scholé's HR dashboard turns AI training into numbers your CFO respects: adoption rate, mastery level, and time to skill for every team and every tool — trackable in natural language from day one. See the metric your L&D budget has been missing before you scroll another inch.

sections.headline: Completion certificates don't survive a budget meeting.
sections.body: Most L&D teams can show who finished a course. Almost none can show who changed how they work. That gap is why learning budgets get cut, and why L&D is not in the room when priorities get set. 83% of HR leaders believe their company supports AI learning — only 64% of employees agree, and half your workforce says workload leaves no room for training at all. Meanwhile the licenses renew and the productivity gains stay theoretical.

sections.body: Ask questions in plain language and get answers about real adoption for every team and every tool, ready before your next QBR. This is the ROI defense L&D directors have been asking for — not who finished, but who changed how they work.

sections.headline: Bring numbers to your next budget meeting
sections.body: 20 minutes, live with a founder. We'll show the dashboard on data like yours and leave you with a metric framework you can use either way. Bring your adoption numbers if you have them and we'll show you what closing the gap looks like.

currentVariants.name: v1-roi-frontloaded
currentVariants.ctaGoal: Book a 20-minute live demo with a founder (L&D/HR buyer)
currentVariants.thesis: The evidence is unambiguous: every variant bleeds out at the hero, the CTA is functionally unreachable (single-digit views everywhere), and the content that actually converts — features (v1 sentiment 1.17, best-read section in the dataset) and tour proof-points — sits buried below heroes most visitors abandon. roi_proof is the universal #1 conversion killer yet its proof section is starved of traffic (v1: 96/1392) and lands flat. This page makes one strategic bet: front-load the ROI proof and the highest-sentiment feature content into the top third of the page, anchor everything to the ld_director buyer (the most reliable converter: v4 10/238, v1 8/289), and shorten the path to the CTA so satisfied visitors who say 'I'd book a demo' actually reach the ask. We keep v1's winning hero (sentiment 0.45, the only hero that carried real traffic deep), its problem section (sentiment 0.55), its features (1.17) and its high-sentiment CTA (1.08), and we cut the dead weight: the how section (-0.08), the tour (-0.11), the press wall (-0.08, exit 62%), and the FAQ (-0.06) that pushed the CTA out of reach. We quantify ROI in the hero and lift the Decathlon proof up to sit directly against the ROI claim rather than gating it behind seven sections.

changelog.what: Quantified ROI language and buyer-anchoring pulled into the hero, kept v1's proven hero copy and CTA-to-dashboard framing.
changelog.why: Hero quality directly predicts downstream survival, and v1's hero was the best in the set, carrying 833 visitors deep and delivering the most conversions.
changelog.evidence: v1 hero sentiment 0.45, exit 38% (lowest in set), carried 833 to 'how' and 270 to features vs. negative-hero collapses (v0 -0.21/19 conv, v4 -0.12/20 conv, v2 -0.31/0 conv).

changelog.what: Lifted the Decathlon/Harvard proof section from position 6 up to position 3, directly against the ROI claim.
changelog.why: roi_proof is the universal dominant conversion killer and the proof that would answer it is starved of traffic and buried below content most visitors never reach.
changelog.evidence: roi_proof was #1 or #2 unresolved in all six variants (v1 lost 484); proof section reached only 96/1392 on v1 and 31/740 on v0.

changelog.what: Kept v1's features section nearly intact and moved it into the top third of the page.
changelog.why: Features is the emotional peak of the page and the best-read section in the entire dataset, but was gated behind sections most people abandoned.
changelog.evidence: v1 features scored sentiment 1.17 at readRate 0.44 — best-read anywhere — yet only 270/1392 (19%) reached it.

changelog.what: Cut the how_it_works, product_tour, and press-wall sections; trimmed the FAQ to two high-frequency items.
changelog.why: These were the low-sentiment, high-exit sections pushing the CTA out of reach; the CTA was functionally unreachable across every variant.
changelog.evidence: v1 how sentiment -0.08/exit 47%, tour -0.11/exit 53%, press -0.08/exit 62%, faq -0.06/exit 56%; CTA views single-digit everywhere (v1=11, v0=3, v2=1).

changelog.what: Reduced the page from 10 sections to 7 and kept the demo-gated 'Book a demo' CTA rather than switching to self-serve.
changelog.why: Satisfied visitors were leaving before reaching an unreachable CTA — shortening the path fixes the friction; self-serve unlocked learners but sacrificed the buyer conversions this page is built around.
changelog.evidence: LOST visitors repeatedly said 'addressed 2/2 of my critical concerns. I'd book a demo'; v5's self-serve dropped overall conversion to 1.9% (16/835) with ld_director falling to 3/190.

changelog.what: Anchored all messaging to the L&D/HR director buyer instead of diffusing across six personas.
changelog.why: ld_director is the most reliable converter and diffuse messaging dilutes this without compensating gain.
changelog.evidence: ld_director converts best across variants (v4 10/238, v1 8/289, v0 5/187); v5/v3's six-persona diffusion lowered fitness (v5 25.5).

This page makes one bet: anchor on the L&D director (the most reliable buyer: v4 10/238, v1 8/289, v0 5/187) with v1's best-performing hero (sentiment 0.45, exit 38%, carried 833 to 'how'), then hoist the ROI dashboard proof and features/tour value ABOVE the fold-abandonment line so roi_proof is answered before visitors quit, and shorten the path so the CTA — repeatedly strongly positive (v1 1.08, v5 1.23) but seen by single digits (v1=11, v0=3) — is actually reachable by satisfied visitors who kept saying 'addressed 2/2, I'd book a demo' yet never arrived at the ask.

changelog.why: The press section was a consistent dead weight, and the raw-question FAQ landed negative; integration and compliance still need honest coverage.

changelog.what: Shortened the page to 7 sections and kept v1's high-performing 'book a demo' CTA reachable near the top of the scroll depth most visitors reach.
changelog.why: Satisfied visitors were leaving because the CTA was functionally unreachable; a shorter path lets intent reach the ask.

changelog.what: Anchored the entire page on the L&D director rather than diffusing across six personas.
changelog.why: The L&D director is the most reliable buyer, and diffused messaging dilutes this without compensating gain.
changelog.evidence: ld_director converts best across variants (v4 10/238, v1 8/289, v0 5/187); v5's diffused self-serve approach dropped overall conversion to 1.9% and fitness 25.5.

sections.headline: Stop reporting completion rates. Start proving AI adoption.
sections.body: Scholé's HR dashboard turns AI training into numbers your CFO respects: adoption rate, mastery level, and time to skill for every team and every tool, trackable in natural language from day one. Ask it a question, get the metric before your next QBR.

sections.headline: One of the most business-oriented HR dashboards I have encountered
sections.body: Two organizations that hold learning to a high bar already run on Scholé — and the ROI signal is the dashboard itself, not a completion certificate.

items.title: Decathlon Switzerland
items.detail: "It has significantly improved perceptions of AI at Decathlon Switzerland... The HR dashboard is one of the most business-oriented I have encountered. A true asset for any L&D leader whose role is increasingly evolving to align more closely with business priorities." — Raphaëlle Rey, Leader of People Development

items.title: Harvard Data Science Initiative
items.detail: "We work with the Scholé team to teach Harvard Data Science Initiative students online, and there is simply no comparison. They are the best."

sections.headline: Insights your LMS admin can't give you
sections.body: Ask questions in plain language and get answers about real adoption for every team and every tool, ready before your next budget meeting. This is the section visitors read most and rate highest — so it comes early.

items.title: Adapts in real time
items.detail: Lesson content adjusts as each learner progresses.

items.title: Content tied to daily work
items.detail: Every lesson maps to the learner's actual tools — Notion, Excel, PowerPoint — and job function.

items.title: Measurable outcomes
items.detail: Completion rates, mastery levels, and adoption metrics, trackable in natural language from day one.

sections.headline: Completion certificates don't survive a budget meeting.
sections.body: Most L&D teams can show who finished a course. Almost none can show who changed how they work. That gap is why learning budgets get cut, and why L&D is not in the room when priorities get set. Meanwhile 83% of HR leaders believe their company supports AI learning — only 64% of employees agree, and half say workload leaves no room for training at all.

sections.headline: What your employees actually get
sections.body: Ask Olé, an AI tutor team that adjusts to each learner — never too easy, never too hard. Skill scores updated after every lesson, XP and level-ups that reward consistency. 2-minute micro-lessons on mobile, desktop, text, audio, and video that fit inside the workday.

sections.headline: Common questions
sections.body: The objections that stopped short of the demo, answered before we ask.

items.title: Fits your existing stack
items.detail: Scholé integrates with existing LMS and HRIS systems, so it layers onto what you already run rather than replacing it.

items.title: Different from Coursera, LinkedIn Learning, and ChatGPT
items.detail: Not a course catalog and not a raw chatbot — a multi-agent engine that builds a role-specific path per employee and measures adoption.

items.title: EU AI Act
items.detail: Scholé helps you prepare for EU AI Act literacy requirements as part of adoption, not as the headline reason to train.

sections.headline: Bring numbers to your next budget meeting
sections.body: 20 minutes, live with a founder. Bring your adoption numbers if you have them and we'll show the dashboard on data like yours — and leave you with a metric framework you can use either way. Your licenses renew regardless; this is what closing the gap looks like.
